content: Hey Saturday, we're designing a push notification system for our mobile app and I'm trying to figure out the best approach. We've got maybe 500k users across iOS and Android. I'm thinking we should use Firebase Cloud Messaging for Android since we already use Firebase, but I'm less sure about iOS. What's your recommendation for the overall architecture?

That's a solid starting point with FCM for Android — you're already integrated with Firebase so that makes operational sense. For iOS, you'll definitely want to use APNs (Apple Push Notification service) since that's the only official channel Apple allows. The good news is that both FCM and APNs are pretty mature, so you won't have major reliability surprises. The typical architecture would be: your backend service publishes notifications to both platforms with their respective credentials and certificates, then each platform handles delivery independently. One thing to keep in mind — APNs can be finicky with certificate management; you'll need to set up proper certificate rotation and monitoring, which adds some operational overhead. For 500k users, you're at a scale where you should definitely implement a retry mechanism for failed deliveries, which we can talk through more.

Thanks, that makes sense. So if a notification fails to send to a user on iOS, we need a system to retry it, right? I'm imagining something like a queue that stores failed attempts. How would you structure that? Should we store which users got which notifications, or just track failures?

Great question — you actually need to track both. Here's why: you need to know which user failed to receive which notification, because APNs and FCM can fail for different reasons. Some failures are permanent (user uninstalled the app, invalid token) while others are temporary (network hiccup, server overload). I'd recommend a retry queue that stores at minimum: the user ID, the notification content, the target platform, the timestamp of the attempt, and the failure reason if available. Then implement exponential backoff — retry after 5 minutes, then 15 minutes, then an hour, etc. You can cap retries at maybe 3-5 attempts before giving up. Also, you should purge really old entries to avoid queue bloat. One extra consideration: store the device token with the attempt too, because tokens can be refreshed and you want to know which token was actually invalid when a send fails.

That makes sense. Now, what about user preferences? Like some users probably don't want all notifications, maybe they want only urgent ones or they want to disable marketing notifications. Where should we store that and how do we handle it in the send logic?

Notification preferences should definitely be stored per-user in your user database — typically as a settings document or a dedicated preferences table. I'd structure it something like: notification_type (urgent, marketing, recommendations, etc.), enabled boolean, and maybe a quiet hours setting (like no notifications between 10pm-8am). Then in your send logic, before you even attempt to queue a notification, you check the user's preferences first. If they have notifications disabled for that type, just skip sending entirely — don't even queue it. This is more efficient than queuing and retrying something that will never be delivered. One important thing: make sure your backend can quickly lookup and check these preferences; you might want to cache them with a short TTL if you're sending a lot of notifications simultaneously. Also, give users a way to update these preferences through both the mobile app and potentially a web dashboard. And definitely log when notifications are skipped due to user preferences so you can analyze why users are disabling certain notification types.

Good points. One more thing — we'll probably be sending thousands of notifications per minute during certain campaigns, so we need to make sure we're not overwhelming either platform. Should we rate limit our sends to FCM and APNs, or do they handle that themselves?

Great practical question. Both platforms have rate limits and quotas, and while they'll tell you if you exceed them, you absolutely want to implement rate limiting on your side rather than discovering it when sends start failing. FCM has generous quotas (typically millions of messages per day) but APNs is stricter — Apple doesn't officially publish exact limits but generally allows high volumes if you spread them out. The smart approach is to implement a token bucket or leaky bucket rate limiter in your notification service. Maybe something like: allow 10,000 sends per second for FCM and 5,000 per second for APNs, with the ability to tune those numbers based on what you observe. Beyond rate limiting, also batch your requests efficiently — both platforms accept batch send APIs, so you can send 100 notifications in a single HTTP request rather than individual requests, which dramatically reduces overhead. Monitor your send latency and error rates closely; if you start seeing rejections or timeouts, that's a signal to back off. During your campaign peaks, queue notifications if you're approaching limits rather than immediately failing them — that's where your retry queue becomes critical.

This is really helpful. Just to confirm I understand the flow: user triggers an event, we check their preferences, build the notification, rate limit it if needed, send to FCM/APNs, and if it fails, retry from our queue. But what happens when we actually deliver? How do we know the notification made it to the user's device?

That's the trickier part, honestly. Both FCM and APNs will tell you if they accepted the notification (i.e., it made it into their system), but neither platform directly confirms delivery to the actual device — they just do their best to deliver. FCM is a bit better here because it returns a message ID you can track, and it provides some feedback if the token is invalid or the app was uninstalled. APNs is more opaque; you get a response code that tells you if Apple accepted it, but not whether the device actually received it. The reality is that the best you can do is trust that the platform delivered it if they accept your request. However, you can implement optional client-side confirmation: when the app opens or at startup, you can query your backend to say "hey, what notifications should I have received?" and compare against what the device actually has. This helps you catch scenarios where notifications were silently lost. For your retry queue, I'd suggest: if the platform rejects it (invalid token, uninstalled app), don't retry; but if the platform accepts it but you get no confirmation after a certain time window, retry. Just be careful not to bombard users with duplicate notifications if both attempts succeed.

Okay, one last thing before we move to implementation. We're thinking about starting simple — just urgent notifications initially. Should we design the whole system now to support multiple notification types (marketing, recommendations, etc.) from the start, or build it just for urgent and iterate later?

I'd recommend designing for multiple types from the start, even if you only implement urgent initially. The reason is that your infrastructure (the retry queue, preference storage, rate limiting) doesn't become significantly more complex by making it type-aware. It's actually easier to build it properly once than to retrofit it later when you're already running in production. Specifically: when you define your notification model, include a type field. When you build preference storage, make it flexible enough to handle multiple types. The rate limiter and retry logic don't really care what type of notification they're handling — it's mostly a parameter. So your initial implementation could literally just be: create notifications with type='urgent', allow all users to receive urgent by default, and the infrastructure is ready for when you add marketing or other types. However, I wouldn't recommend building the full UI for users to configure multiple notification preferences if you're only sending one type — launch with urgent, get it stable, then add user preference controls when you're ready to launch the second type. That way you validate demand and user behavior before investing in UX complexity.

Perfect, this gives us a really solid plan. Before we start coding, let me just make sure about one technical detail: when we store the retry queue, should that be in the same database as our user data, or should it be separate? We're using PostgreSQL for the main app database but I'm wondering if a different store might be better for the queue.

Good thinking. You can definitely store it in PostgreSQL alongside your user data — that's the simplest approach and totally workable at your scale. The main consideration is query patterns: you'll be frequently checking the queue for entries that need retrying (based on timestamp and failure count), so make sure you have proper indexes on created_at and next_retry_time columns. You might also want a separate table just for the queue to keep it clean and performant as it grows. That said, if you anticipate the queue growing to millions of entries (which could happen with large campaigns), you might consider a more specialized solution like Redis for the queue itself. Redis is great for this because it's fast, you can set automatic TTLs on entries to prevent bloat, and it's easy to implement the retry logic with polling or pub/sub patterns. But honestly, start with PostgreSQL first — it's simpler operationally if you're not running a separate Redis instance. You can always migrate to Redis later if queue performance becomes a bottleneck. Just monitor queue size and latency as you go live, and be ready to migrate if needed. For now, PostgreSQL with good indexing will serve you well.
